content.problemStatement:
There is an N×NN\times NN×N grid, with rows numbered 111 to NNN from top to bottom and columns numbered 111 to NNN from left to right. The cell at the intersection of row iii and column jjj is denoted (i,j)(i, j)(i,j).

Chef is standing at cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) of the grid, and would like to get to cell (N,N)(N, N)(N,N). To do this, he can move one step up/down/left/right from the cell he's standing at, as long as he remains within the grid.

That is, from cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) he can move to one of {(i−1,j),(i+1,j),(i,j−1),(i,j+1)}\{(i-1, j), (i+1, j), (i, j-1), (i, j+1)\}{(i−1,j),(i+1,j),(i,j−1),(i,j+1)}.

Unfortunately, there are snakes in the grid!

Each snake has an initial cell (xi,yi)(x_i, y_i)(xi​,yi​), and a direction did_idi​. The direction is one of `'U'`, `'D'`, `'L'`, or `'R'` - denoting up, down, left, and right respectively.  This snake acts as follows:


In the beginning, it occupies only point (xi,yi)(x_i, y_i)(xi​,yi​).
At the **end** of each second, it will extend one unit in its respective direction.

For example, if (xi,yi)=(4,7)(x_i, y_i) = (4, 7)(xi​,yi​)=(4,7) and di=Ld_i = \texttt{L}di​=L, then:

At first, this snake occupies (4,7)(4, 7)(4,7)
After one second, it occupies (4,7)(4, 7)(4,7) and (4,6)(4, 6)(4,6) (i.e, it occupies one point more to the left).
After another second, it occupies (4,7),(4,6),(4, 7), (4, 6), (4,7),(4,6), and (4,5)(4, 5)(4,5); and so on.


Snakes stop extending when they reach the boundary of the grid.

Multiple snakes are allowed to occupy the same cell.

Chef calls a point dangerous if **at least** one snake occupies it.

The danger of a path from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (N,N)(N, N)(N,N) is the number of dangerous points it contains.

Help Chef answer QQQ queries of the following form:


Given TTT, what's the minimum danger of a path from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (N,N)(N, N)(N,N) at the **start** of time TTT?

Input Format

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ — the size of the grid and the number of queries.
The next NNN lines describe the grid. The iii-th of them contains a string SiS_iSi​ of length NNN, denoting row iii.
Each character of the string is one of five from `".LRUD"`, where `'.'` denotes an empty cell and the other four denote a snake at that cell with the specified direction.
The next QQQ lines describe the queries. The iii-th of them contains a single integer TiT_iTi​, the time for the iii-th query.


Output Format
For each test case, output QQQ lines. The iii-th of them should contain a single integer: the answer to the iii-th query.

Explanation:
The initial grid looks as follows:

with one possible path from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (5,5)(5, 5)(5,5) marked out, that has danger 000.
This is the answer to the first query.
The second query has T=3T = 3T=3, meaning we're at the start of the third second. All snakes have moved twice (recall that snakes move at the end of a second). One possible path with danger 111 now is:

The third query is T=6T = 6T=6, so all snakes have moved five times. One optimal path with danger 333 is:

Note that the cells (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) and/or (N,N)(N, N)(N,N) may themselves be dangerous, in which case they must be counted into the answer as well.
